misconceptionSummary: Students confuse perimeter (length of boundary) with area (space inside).

visuals.ruleText: Perimeter is a length — measured in cm, m, km. Area is a surface — measured in cm², m², km².
visuals.subText: Perimeter goes around the edge; area fills the inside

steps.narration: Perimeter and area are completely different measurements. Perimeter measures the length of the boundary — one-dimensional. Area measures the surface enclosed — two-dimensional. The units confirm which you have: cm for perimeter, cm squared for area.

lines.text: Rectangle A: 2 × 6 = 12 cm² area, perimeter = 16 cm
Rectangle B: 3 × 4 = 12 cm² area, perimeter = 14 cm

steps.narration: Two shapes can have identical areas but very different perimeters. A two by six rectangle and a three by four rectangle both have twelve square centimetres inside, but their boundaries are different lengths.

Always write the units. If your answer is in centimetres, you have found a perimeter. If it is in square centimetres, you have found an area. Mismatched units are a reliable signal that the wrong formula was used.

visuals.expression: Perimeter = sum of side lengths (cm, m…)
visuals.label: Perimeter = boundary length; area = enclosed surface

steps.narration: Perimeter is a length — always add the side lengths and write the answer in linear units. Never square them.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: giving a perimeter answer in cm² or calculating area instead. Perimeter is a length — add side lengths and write the answer in cm or m, not cm².
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Perimeter measures the boundary, not the interior. The answer is in cm or m — never cm². If you see cm², you have calculated area instead.
